iron-deficiency-anemia.label: Iron deficiency anemia pattern
iron-deficiency-anemia.summary: Anemia with biochemical or pattern evidence of iron deficiency. Ferritin below the age/menstrual threshold is the strongest built-in criterion.
defaultNextSteps: Identify the source: dietary insufficiency, excessive cow milk, menstrual/GI/other blood loss, malabsorption, or increased requirement.
Review ferritin in inflammatory context and use local assay units/reference intervals.
If the course is atypical or response is inadequate, reassess adherence, ongoing blood loss, malabsorption, inflammation, thalassemia, lead, and alternative diagnoses.

iron-deficiency-without-anemia.label: Iron deficiency without anemia pattern
iron-deficiency-without-anemia.summary: Ferritin meets the AAP iron-deficiency threshold, but hemoglobin is not below the supplied age/sex or local lower limit.
defaultNextSteps: Assess dietary, menstrual, gastrointestinal, blood donation, and malabsorption contributors.
Interpret in the context of inflammation and local assay methods.

anemia-of-inflammation.label: Anemia of inflammation / inflammatory iron restriction pattern
anemia-of-inflammation.summary: Anemia associated with inflammation and an iron-restricted pattern in which ferritin may be normal or elevated.
defaultNextSteps: Confirm and characterize the inflammatory condition and trend CBC, ferritin, CRP/ESR, transferrin saturation, and TIBC using local interpretation.
Assess for coexisting absolute iron deficiency; a low ferritin remains important even with inflammation.

thalassemia-pattern.label: Thalassemia trait or other globin-production pattern
thalassemia-pattern.summary: Persistent microcytosis with non-low ferritin and supporting CBC, smear, newborn-screen, family, or hemoglobin-analysis findings.
defaultNextSteps: Review newborn-screen results and family/ancestry history.
Use hemoglobin analysis/HPLC for beta-chain variants and age-appropriate beta-thalassemia evaluation.
For suspected alpha thalassemia outside the newborn period, recognize that routine electrophoresis may be normal and consider alpha-globin testing.
Correct coexisting iron deficiency before using a normal HbA2 to exclude beta-thalassemia trait.

beta-thalassemia-pattern.label: Beta-thalassemia pattern
beta-thalassemia-pattern.summary: Age-appropriate elevated HbA2 or beta-globin testing supports a beta-thalassemia diagnosis/trait pattern.
defaultNextSteps: Confirm with age-appropriate hemoglobin analysis and/or molecular testing when clinically indicated.
Evaluate for coexisting iron deficiency because it can lower HbA2 and complicate interpretation.

alpha-thalassemia-pattern.label: Alpha-thalassemia pattern
alpha-thalassemia-pattern.summary: Hemoglobin Bart’s on the newborn screen or alpha-globin testing supports alpha thalassemia.
defaultNextSteps: Review the newborn screen and consider alpha-globin molecular testing when persistent microcytosis remains unexplained.
Do not rely on a normal routine electrophoresis outside the newborn period to exclude alpha thalassemia.

lead-exposure-associated-anemia.label: Lead exposure requiring a lead clinical pathway
lead-exposure-associated-anemia.summary: A blood lead level at or above the CDC blood lead reference value requires exposure follow-up; lead may coexist with iron deficiency and can contribute to anemia.
defaultNextSteps: If the elevated result is capillary, obtain confirmatory venous testing on the CDC timeline.
Take an environmental exposure history and involve public health/environmental services as indicated.
Assess and address coexisting iron deficiency.

sideroblastic-iron-loading-microcytosis.label: Sideroblastic or iron-loading microcytic process
sideroblastic-iron-loading-microcytosis.summary: Microcytosis with high iron/ferritin and compatible smear or exposure findings requires specialist evaluation for congenital/acquired sideroblastic processes and mimics.
defaultNextSteps: Review medications, toxins, nutrition, congenital history, iron studies, and smear with pediatric hematology.
Do not diagnose sideroblastic anemia from basophilic stippling alone; consider lead and other causes.

blood-loss.label: Acute or chronic blood-loss pattern
blood-loss.summary: Bleeding history with an appropriate reticulocyte response, or iron deficiency with a chronic bleeding source, supports blood loss.
defaultNextSteps: Localize and quantify bleeding: menstrual, gastrointestinal, epistaxis, trauma/procedure, blood donation, urinary, or other.
Assess hemodynamic status and coagulation/bleeding-disorder history when indicated.

hemolysis-unspecified.label: Hemolytic anemia pattern—etiology not yet specified
hemolysis-unspecified.summary: Anemia with elevated reticulocyte response and biochemical or clinical evidence of red-cell destruction.
defaultNextSteps: Review smear, DAT, bilirubin fractionation, LDH, haptoglobin, urinalysis, medication/exposure history, family history, and newborn-screen/hemoglobinopathy data.
Interpret haptoglobin and other markers in age, liver, inflammatory, and specimen context.

autoimmune-hemolytic-anemia.label: Immune hemolytic anemia pattern
autoimmune-hemolytic-anemia.summary: A positive direct antiglobulin test in a hemolytic pattern supports immune-mediated hemolysis, subject to transfusion and assay context.
defaultNextSteps: Have the DAT pattern and smear reviewed with hematology/transfusion medicine.
Assess recent transfusion, infection, immune disease, and medication context.

hereditary-spherocytosis.label: Hereditary spherocytosis pattern
hereditary-spherocytosis.summary: DAT-negative hemolysis with spherocytes, splenomegaly, neonatal jaundice, or family history is compatible with hereditary spherocytosis.
defaultNextSteps: Confirm with an appropriate specialized red-cell membrane test (for example EMA binding) and hematology interpretation.
Review transfusion timing because it can affect specialized testing.

g6pd-deficiency.label: G6PD deficiency pattern
g6pd-deficiency.summary: Oxidant-associated hemolysis, bite/blister cells, or a deficient quantitative G6PD result supports G6PD deficiency.
defaultNextSteps: Use a quantitative G6PD assay interpreted for sex, reticulocytosis, transfusion, and assay method.
If a result is normal during acute hemolysis or soon after transfusion but suspicion remains, repeat testing at baseline with hematology guidance.

microangiopathic-hemolysis.label: Microangiopathic or mechanical hemolysis pattern
microangiopathic-hemolysis.summary: Schistocytes with hemolysis may indicate microangiopathic or mechanical red-cell destruction; thrombocytopenia, renal, or neurologic findings increase urgency.
defaultNextSteps: Urgently correlate with platelets, renal function, neurologic findings, coagulation studies, blood pressure, infection history, and expert smear review.

sickling-hemoglobinopathy.label: Sickling hemoglobinopathy pattern
sickling-hemoglobinopathy.summary: Known newborn-screen disease, confirmatory hemoglobin analysis, or sickled cells supports a sickling hemoglobinopathy pathway.
defaultNextSteps: Verify newborn-screen and confirmatory hemoglobin analysis/genotype.
Use disease-specific pediatric hematology pathways for acute symptoms and longitudinal care.

malaria-or-parasitic-hemolysis.label: Malaria or travel-associated hemolysis pattern
malaria-or-parasitic-hemolysis.summary: Fever, relevant travel/residence, and anemia/hemolysis require prompt pathogen-specific evaluation.
defaultNextSteps: Obtain urgent travel history and appropriate malaria testing according to local infectious-disease protocols; repeat testing may be required when suspicion is high.

renal-anemia.label: Renal/EPO-limited anemia pattern
renal-anemia.summary: Normocytic anemia with an inappropriately low reticulocyte response and kidney disease/renal dysfunction supports a renal production-limited pattern.
defaultNextSteps: Review renal function, urinalysis, blood pressure, inflammation, iron status, nutrition, and nephrology context.

transient-erythroblastopenia.label: Transient erythroblastopenia of childhood pattern
transient-erythroblastopenia.summary: An isolated, usually normocytic anemia with reticulocytopenia in a young child, often after a viral illness, may fit transient erythroblastopenia after exclusion of dangerous alternatives.
defaultNextSteps: Confirm isolated red-cell suppression, trend reticulocytes/CBC, and exclude congenital marrow failure, leukemia, renal/inflammatory disease, and parvovirus in a chronic hemolytic disorder.

parvovirus-aplastic-crisis.label: Parvovirus-associated aplastic crisis pattern
parvovirus-aplastic-crisis.summary: Abrupt anemia with reticulocytopenia in a patient with a chronic hemolytic disorder is compatible with parvovirus-associated aplastic crisis.
defaultNextSteps: Urgently involve pediatric hematology and obtain parvovirus testing using the local acute-care pathway.

marrow-failure-infiltration.label: Bone marrow failure, infiltration, or malignancy pattern
marrow-failure-infiltration.summary: Reticulocytopenia with additional cytopenias, blasts, organomegaly, lymphadenopathy, or constitutional/exam red flags requires urgent evaluation for marrow failure or infiltration.
defaultNextSteps: Obtain urgent expert review of CBC differential and smear; coordinate pediatric hematology/oncology evaluation and further testing.
Do not delay escalation because anemia is mild if blasts or multiple cytopenias are present.

b12-folate-deficiency.label: Vitamin B12 or folate deficiency pattern
b12-folate-deficiency.summary: Macrocytosis with low B12/folate or hypersegmented neutrophils supports a megaloblastic pathway.
defaultNextSteps: Confirm the deficient nutrient and investigate diet, malabsorption, medications, autoimmune disease, and associated neurologic findings.
Consider mixed iron deficiency when RDW is high or MCV is not frankly elevated.

copper-deficiency.label: Copper deficiency pattern
copper-deficiency.summary: Low copper with anemia, especially with neutropenia or malabsorption/enteral nutrition risk, supports copper deficiency.
defaultNextSteps: Confirm copper status and evaluate zinc exposure, malabsorption, intestinal surgery, and nutrition support with specialist input.

thyroid-liver-medication-macrocytosis.label: Non-megaloblastic macrocytosis pattern
thyroid-liver-medication-macrocytosis.summary: Thyroid disease, liver disease, reticulocytosis, or medication exposure may produce macrocytosis without primary B12/folate deficiency.
defaultNextSteps: Review TSH, liver tests, reticulocyte response, medication list, alcohol/toxin exposure where age-appropriate, and smear.

inherited-marrow-failure.label: Inherited bone-marrow-failure syndrome pattern
inherited-marrow-failure.summary: Early-onset macrocytic/production-limited anemia, congenital anomalies, short stature, skin pigmentation, limb anomalies, or progressive cytopenias raise inherited marrow-failure syndromes such as Diamond-Blackfan or Fanconi anemia.
defaultNextSteps: Refer to pediatric hematology/genetics for syndrome-specific marrow, chromosome-breakage, ribosomal, and germline evaluation.
Avoid assigning a specific syndrome from phenotype alone.

mixed-anemia-or-transfusion-effect.label: Mixed anemia or recent-transfusion effect
mixed-anemia-or-transfusion-effect.summary: Coexisting deficiencies, reticulocytosis, inflammation, or recent transfusion can normalize or broaden MCV/RDW and obscure a single morphology.
defaultNextSteps: Interpret trends and pre-transfusion CBC/smear when available; assess iron, B12/folate, inflammation, reticulocytes, and transfusion timing.

iron-refractory-iron-deficiency-anemia.label: Iron-refractory iron-deficiency anemia or rare iron-handling disorder pattern
iron-refractory-iron-deficiency-anemia.summary: Persistent microcytic iron-restricted anemia that does not follow the expected course after adherence, blood loss, malabsorption, inflammation, and thalassemia are addressed may require evaluation for rare iron-handling disorders.
defaultNextSteps: First verify dose/adherence, diagnosis, ongoing blood loss, malabsorption, inflammation, and coexisting hemoglobinopathy.
If unexplained and persistent, involve pediatric hematology for specialized iron/hepcidin and genetic evaluation.
